turns.content: I need to write a short speech. Nothing long. Maybe two minutes. For a graduation.
I can help with that. A couple questions to get started -- what kind of graduation is this, and what's your role? Are you the speaker, a parent, or something else?
My son is graduating the fire academy on March 22nd. They asked me to say a few words because I'm a battalion chief with PFD. 30 years on the job. But this is about him not about me.

That's a proud moment -- your son graduating the academy while you're a 30-year veteran. And you're right to keep the focus on him and his class. A two-minute speech is about 250-300 words. Here's a structure that would work well:

1. **Brief intro** -- who you are, acknowledge the occasion
2. **Address the graduates** -- what the job really means, something honest they won't hear in training
3. **Personal touch** -- a line or two about your son without making the whole thing about him
4. **Close strong** -- welcome them to the brotherhood, keep it short

Want me to draft something? What tone are you going for -- formal, or more real and direct?
Real and direct. I don't do formal. These kids need to hear the truth not a bunch of inspirational quotes off the internet.

Got it. Here's a draft:

---

"For those who don't know me, I'm Battalion Chief Patrick O'Brien. I've been with the Philadelphia Fire Department for 30 years. Today one of these graduates is my son, so I'm standing up here as a chief and a father, and I'll try to keep it together for both.

You earned this. Nobody gave it to you. The academy is designed to find out who quits and who doesn't, and you didn't quit. That matters. Remember that on the bad days.

Here's what they didn't teach you in there. The job is not what you think it is. It's better and it's worse. You'll see things you can't unsee. You'll carry people out of the worst day of their life and most of them won't remember your name. That's fine. You didn't sign up for the thank-yous.

Lean on your crew. That's not a suggestion. The person next to you is the reason you come home. Trust them. Have their back. Every single time.

Welcome to the job. Make us proud."

---

About 170 words, just under two minutes. Want me to adjust anything?
That's pretty close. Take out the part about trying to keep it together. I'm not saying that in front of 200 people.

Fair enough. Here's the revised version:

---

"For those who don't know me, I'm Battalion Chief Patrick O'Brien. I've been with the Philadelphia Fire Department for 30 years. Today one of these graduates is my son, so I'm standing up here as a chief and a father.

You earned this. Nobody gave it to you. The academy is designed to find out who quits and who doesn't, and you didn't quit. That matters. Remember that on the bad days.

Here's what they didn't teach you in there. The job is not what you think it is. It's better and it's worse. You'll see things you can't unsee. You'll carry people out of the worst day of their life and most of them won't remember your name. That's fine. You didn't sign up for the thank-yous.

Lean on your crew. That's not a suggestion. The person next to you is the reason you come home. Trust them. Have their back. Every single time.

Welcome to the job. Make us proud."

---

Clean and direct. Anything else you'd change?
No that's good. Marie's been carrying a pack of tissues in her purse since we got the date. She'll be a wreck before they even call his name.
Ha, can't blame her -- watching your kid follow in your footsteps like that is a big deal. Sounds like it's going to be a great day for the whole family.
I'll be in dress uniform. Full class A's. Haven't worn them since the memorial service last year. Time to make sure they still fit.
Good idea to try them on ahead of time -- you don't want to find out the morning of that something doesn't button. A week out gives you time to get anything pressed or adjusted if needed.
Copy that. I'll pull them out tonight. Thanks for the speech. Kept it short and honest. That's all I wanted.
You're welcome. It'll land well because it sounds like you. Congratulations to your son -- and to you. March 22nd is going to be a good day.
Anytime. Good luck up there, Chief.
